checks.a_analysis: The JSON output contains the headings, addresses, body paragraphs, and table contents exactly as they appear in the source document. No fabricated sentences or sections were detected. AI-generated table captions and summaries are explicitly allowed as acceptable changes for accessibility purposes.

Every piece of contact information in the JSON output, including the mailing addresses for both the Office of Administrative Hearings and the recipient, as well as the phone and fax numbers for all six administrative divisions in the footer table, was cross-referenced with the source document. All numbers (e.g., Administration 919/431-3000, fax: 919/431-3100) and zip codes (e.g., 27699-6714) match the source perfectly.

The JSON correctly captures content from both pages of the source document. Page 1's letter content and footer table are present, and Page 2's RRC Determination list and rule table are fully represented.

There are two tables. 1) The footer contact table on page 1 has 6 logical divisions; the JSON captures all 6 as headers with their corresponding data. 2) The rule determination table on page 2 has 8 data rows (Rules .0901 through .1502); the JSON captures all 8 rows without omission.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: All semantic text from the body paragraphs and headings is present. While the diagonal 'DRAFT' watermark present on both source pages is not included in the text elements of the JSON, watermarks are typically considered non-semantic background elements in document conversion, and all other text content is preserved perfectly.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content matches the source document exactly. No wording changes, such as 'shall' to 'may' or alterations of dates/numbers, were observed. Reformatting of the footer contact information into a structured table is considered an acceptable accessibility improvement.

checks.a_analysis: The output document contains no significant content duplication. The 'RRC DETERMINATION' text appears in both the subsection title and as a heading element, but this is a structural metadata redundancy rather than a duplication of the body text within the reading flow. Captions and summaries for tables are added for accessibility, which is an explicitly allowed change.

The document uses a consistent heading hierarchy. It starts at H2 for the document header and uses H3 for subsections and headings on the second page. While it lacks an H1 within the elements (the primary title is correctly placed in the metadata), there are no skips in levels (e.g., H2 to H4), which meets the criteria for fidelity and accessibility normalization.

The tables in the output faithfully represent the structure of the source. The contact information footer on page 1 is correctly converted into a multi-column table which reflects its visual layout. The rules determination list on page 2 is also correctly structured as a table with two columns and appropriate headers matching the source.

The reading order follows the logical flow of the letter followed by the attachment. Information proceeds from the header, through the letter body, the signature, the footer contact table, and finally the determination list on the second page. This matches the source document's intended order.
There are no explicit bulleted or numbered lists in the source document. Information presented in columns or rows has been correctly handled via table structures, and the meaning is preserved.

Links are present for rule citations (e.g., 10A NCAC 13J) which appear as underlined/clickable elements in the source PDF. The URLs provided (internal 'rats' host) are consistent with North Carolina administrative documentation. No fabricated or broken external links were found.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output faithfully represents the visual organization of the source document. The header information (addresses, date), recipient block, and body text follow the same flow as the original letter. The multi-column contact information at the bottom of the PDF page 1 is correctly organized into a structured table under a 'Contact Information' heading. The second page's content is appended logically below. The use of a single-column responsive layout is standard for HTML conversion and does not detract from the original intent.

The document contains two main tabular structures. The first is the contact information footer from page 1, which has been rendered as a clean, bordered table in the HTML. The second is the list of rules and determinations from page 2, which is also rendered as a table with clear headers. Both tables are legible, columns are aligned, and the content is easily understandable, maintaining the tabular relationship present in the source.

The meaningful image in the source document—the State Seal of North Carolina—is present and clearly rendered at the top of the HTML output. The 'DRAFT' watermarks visible in the PDF are absent in the HTML; however, per instructions, the omission of decorative watermarks for accessibility reasons is not considered a defect.

A review of the HTML screenshot shows no instances of duplicated body content. While section headings appear in the Table of Contents sidebar, this is a standard navigational feature and is explicitly permitted by the evaluation criteria. The main body content (letter text and tables) appears only once.
